content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

MIT’s NANDA initiative analysed 300 public AI deployments, conducted over 150 executive interviews, and surveyed 350 employees. The finding: 95% of enterprise generative AI projects delivered no measurable financial impact. Not low returns. Not disappointing returns. Zero.
S&P Global found that 42% of companies abandoned most of their AI initiatives in 2025, up sharply from 17% the year before. IBM put the number of initiatives delivering expected ROI at 25%. Morgan Stanley found that only 21% of S&P 500 companies could cite a measurable AI benefit at all.
Spend accelerating. Returns collapsing. And the instinct, almost universally, is to blame the technology.
That instinct is wrong. And understanding why it is wrong is the most practically useful thing anyone navigating the AI moment can do right now.
The technology is not the problem
When an AI project fails and the majority do the post-mortem almost always points at the model. It hallucinated. It was not accurate enough. The outputs were not good enough. We need a better tool.
RAND Corporation’s root-cause analysis of AI failures identifies a consistent set of failure drivers and the most damaging have little to do with algorithms or infrastructure. The recurring causes are: unclear definitions of success, weak data foundations, poor integration into real workflows, chasing technology rather than business outcomes, and fading executive sponsorship.
Not the model. The organisation.
Only a few US employees report that their workplaces have communicated a clear AI strategy. Meanwhile, most of surveyed executives planned to boost their AI spending in the next three years. That gap between spending and strategy, between investment and intention is where the money disappears.
IBM’s senior researcher put it plainly: “People said, ‘Step one: we’re going to use LLMs. Step two: What should we use them for?’” That sequencing: tool first, problem second costs companies millions in lost time and resources.
The measurement problem nobody wants to admit
Here is the failure pattern that appears most consistently across the data, and that is most rarely discussed honestly.
73% of failed AI projects had no agreed definition of success before the project started. 61% of enterprise AI projects were approved on projected ROI that was never measured after launch. The project ships and no one checks whether it worked.
Read that again. Three out of five AI projects are approved based on projected returns that nobody ever goes back to verify. The investment gets made. The tool gets deployed. The dashboard gets built. And the question of whether any of it actually moved the business gets quietly dropped.
Projects with quantified success metrics defined upfront achieve a 54% success rate. Those without achieve just 12%. That is not a marginal difference. That is the difference between a coin flip and a systematic failure. And most organisations are choosing the 12% path, not because they are irrational, but because defining success upfront is harder than deploying a tool and hoping the results speak for themselves.
They rarely do.
The pilot purgatory problem
There is a specific phenomenon that the data keeps returning to, and it has a name: pilot purgatory.
Your proof-of-concept works beautifully in a controlled environment. Then you try to deploy it across the organisation and everything falls apart. 88–95% of AI pilots never reach production. Projects demonstrate initial promise but fail to scale due to organisational readiness gaps, weak business alignment, or technical debt. The primary reasons are organisational, not technical.
The pattern is recognisable to anyone who has watched an AI initiative up close. A team runs a pilot. The pilot shows promising results. Leadership gets excited. The rollout begins. And somewhere between the controlled conditions of the pilot and the messy reality of actual operations real data, real workflows, real people with real resistance then the promise evaporates.
The abandonment rate reflects a fundamental inability to move from experimentation to production. The question that almost nobody asks before starting another pilot is: what is different this time that will get this project into production? If it does not involve data quality, governance, and defined business value, the pilot is just adding to the abandonment statistics.
The data problem underneath everything
Pull on any AI failure and you will eventually find a data problem underneath it.
Some organisations cite data quality and readiness as the number one obstacle to AI success. Fewer report data of sufficient quality and accessibility for AI applications. Through 2026, Gartner predicts that 60% of AI projects unsupported by AI-ready data will be abandoned.
AI does not run on ambition. It runs on data. Specifically, on clean, structured, well-governed data.
Getting data right is the real work. And most organisations treat it as an afterthought.
What the successful minority is doing differently
The 5–26% of organisations generating real returns from AI are not using better models. They are doing something structurally different and the pattern is consistent enough across the data to be instructive.
Before approving any AI investment, they require the business owner to commit to how the value will be captured. If the value is capacity, what will the capacity be redeployed to? If the value is cost reduction, when and how will the cost actually leave the budget? No value capture commitment, no approval.
They treat AI as an operating model change with technology, not a technology deployment that incidentally affects operations. AI that automates a task inside a process designed for humans rarely produces measurable impact. The savings are captured only when the surrounding process, headcount, governance, and metrics are redesigned around it.
Organisations that redesign workflows before selecting AI tools are twice as likely to report significant financial returns. Twice as likely simply by inverting the sequence. Problem first. Tool second.
What this means if you are not a CIO
Most of the conversation about AI ROI is directed at executives and technology leaders. But the failure patterns it describes play out at every level of every organisation and the insight they contain is useful far beyond the boardroom.
The instinct to reach for a tool before defining the problem is not a corporate failure. It shows up in how individuals adopt AI as much as how enterprises do. The person who tries fifteen different AI tools looking for the one that will finally make them productive is running the same pattern as the organisation that launches a dozen pilots looking for the one that will finally move the numbers.
The tool is never the answer to a problem you have not yet defined. Some organisations that experienced AI failure attributed it to expecting too much, too fast, assuming AI would immediately automate complex tasks without the data foundation or change management to make it happen.
Expectation is the gap. Not capability.
That is the real reason AI is not delivering on its promises.
And it is entirely fixable just not in the way the vendors would prefer you to believe.
